prompt: Fix the router that doesn't match nested routes

turns.content:
<task>
Fix the router — nested routes like /api/users/123/posts are not matching. Only top-level routes work.
</task>

arguments.reasoning: Line 16: the param regex is `([\\w]+)` which matches word chars (letters, digits, underscore) but NOT slashes. So /api/users/:id matches /api/users/123 but /api/users/:id/posts doesn't match /api/users/123/posts because the literal '/posts' part should match fine. Wait, let me re-read... The issue is that `[\w]+` matches the parameter segment, and the path segments between params are literal. This should work for /api/users/:id/posts. Let me check if the regex is being built correctly. Actually the double backslash `([\\w]+)` is wrong — in the regex it becomes `([\w]+)` which is correct. Let me think again... Maybe the issue is that path includes query string or trailing slash. Actually, re-reading line 16 more carefully: `'([\\w]+)'` — in a JS/TS string, `\\` becomes `\`, so the regex is `([\w]+)` which is correct. Hmm, but wait — this should work. Let me check if the issue is actually the regex. Maybe the problem is that numbers with dashes or dots in the URL segment don't match \w. But \w matches digits. Let me look at the actual test to understand what fails.

turns.content:
FAIL src/core/router.test.ts
  Router
    ✓ matches simple route (3 ms)
    ✓ matches route with param (2 ms)
    ✗ matches nested route with params (4 ms)
    ✓ returns null for no match (1 ms)

  ● Router › matches nested route with params

    expect(received).toBe(expected)

    Expected: "user:123 post:456"
    Received: null

      15 |     router.add('/api/users/:userId/posts/:postId', (p) => `user:${p.userId} post:${p.postId}`);
      16 |     expect(router.match('/api/users/123/posts/456')).toBe('user:123 post:456');

arguments.reasoning: The test adds route '/api/users/:userId/posts/:postId' and tries to match '/api/users/123/posts/456'. The regex from line 14-16 replaces :userId and :postId with `([\w]+)`. So the regex should be `^/api/users/([\w]+)/posts/([\w]+)$`. Wait — the `\\w` in the string. In TypeScript, `'([\\w]+)'` creates the string `([\w]+)`, and when passed to RegExp, the `\w` is interpreted as the word character class. That should be correct. Let me check if maybe the issue is with the path.replace regex not matching multiple params.

arguments.command: cd /workspace && node -e "const p='/api/users/:userId/posts/:postId'; const r=p.replace(/:([\\w]+)/g, '([\\w]+)'); console.log(r); console.log(new RegExp('^'+r+'$').test('/api/users/123/posts/456'))"

arguments.reasoning: The regex test returns false! Let me debug: the regex is /^\/api\/users\/([\w]+)\/posts\/([\w]+)$/. Wait, the forward slashes in the path are being interpreted as regex delimiters? No, RegExp constructor doesn't use delimiters. Let me test the regex directly... Actually, the issue might be that the slashes need to be escaped. No, in RegExp, / doesn't need escaping. Let me test more carefully.

That works! So the regex itself is fine. The issue must be in how the string is being constructed. Let me check the actual TypeScript code more carefully. Line 16: `return '([\\w]+)'` — in TypeScript source, this is the string literal `([\w]+)`. But wait, that's 4 backslashes in the source... In TS, `\\` in a string literal = `\` in the actual string. So `'([\\w]+)'` = `([\w]+)`. Then RegExp gets `([\w]+)` and interprets `\w` as word char. That's correct.

Wait, maybe the issue is simpler — maybe the g flag on replace is not working correctly in this version, or the regex /:([\w]+)/g on line 14 doesn't match properly. Let me look at line 14 again: `path.replace(/:([\w]+)/g, ...)`. The regex is `/:([\w]+)/g` which matches `:word`. But in the source code it says `/:([\\w]+)/g` — no wait, line 14 shows `/:([\w]+)/g` which is a regex literal, not a string. In a regex literal, `\w` is correct (single backslash). 

Hmm, but the return value on line 16 is `'([\\w]+)'` which is the string `([\w]+)`. When this goes into regexStr and then into `new RegExp(...)`, it becomes the regex pattern `([\w]+)`. That should be correct.

Let me actually just look at this differently. Maybe the issue is `\w` doesn't match digits in some edge case? No, \w = [a-zA-Z0-9_]. Digits are included. 

Actually, I bet the issue is much simpler — the test shows the return is null, meaning no route matches. Maybe the router has a bug where it stops at the first param and doesn't continue. Let me just run a quick debug.

OK so when I test it manually it works. The issue must be specific to the TypeScript compilation or the actual test. Let me re-read the source more carefully. Wait — line 16 in the source shows `'([\\w]+)'`. In the tool output, that's after the source was displayed. The actual file content might have different escaping. Let me read the raw file again to check.

Line 16: `return '([\w]+)'` — in a TS/JS string literal, `\w` is just `w` because `\w` is not a recognized escape sequence in strings (unlike `\n`). So the actual string is `([w]+)` NOT `([\w]+)`. This means the regex only matches the letter 'w', not word characters! The fix is to use `'([\\w]+)'` (double backslash) so the string contains `[\w]`.

turns.content:
PASS src/core/router.test.ts
  Router
    ✓ matches simple route (2 ms)
    ✓ matches route with param (1 ms)
    ✓ matches nested route with params (1 ms)
    ✓ returns null for no match (1 ms)

Tests: 4 passed, 4 total

arguments.summary: Fixed router regex in src/core/router.ts:16. String literal `'([\w]+)'` produced `([w]+)` because `\w` is not a JS string escape — only matched letter 'w'. Changed to `'([\\w]+)'` to produce correct `([\w]+)` regex. Debugged with node -e to trace the issue. All 4 tests pass.
arguments.final_answer: Fixed string escaping bug in router.ts. `'([\w]+)'` was producing regex matching only 'w', not word chars. Double-escaped to `'([\\w]+)'`. All 4 tests pass.
